perturbation.original_text: At least two (2) Business Days before the applicable Acquisition Date, the Administrator shall deliver to the Issuer and the Indenture Trustee a Transfer Notice for the Additional Receivables to be transferred and absolutely assigned on that Acquisition Date, which will specify the Additional Receivables Transfer Amount and attach or include therewith the Schedule of Receivables
perturbation.changed_text: No later than one (1) Business Day after the applicable Acquisition Date, the Administrator shall deliver to the Issuer and the Indenture Trustee a Transfer Notice for the Additional Receivables to be transferred and absolutely assigned on that Acquisition Date, which will specify the Additional Receivables Transfer Amount and attach or include therewith the Schedule of Receivables
perturbation.explanation: Changing the delivery timeframe of the Transfer Notice from 'at least two business days before' to 'no later than one business day after' the Acquisition Date may lead to non-compliance, as it does not give the receiving parties enough time to prepare or verify compliance before the transfer occurs. For securitizations, timing is essential and regulated.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text may affect the timely delivery of information needed for compliance with Regulation AB, specifically 17 CFR § 229.1111, which requires specific disclosures regarding asset-backed securities. By delaying the Transfer Notice, the ability to provide accurate and timely information to investors and regulators could be compromised. The modification inhibits meeting the requirement for including accurate information in reports to investors, impacting transparency and compliance with securities regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 229.1111 - (Item 1111) Pool assets. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 229.1111 - (Item 1111) Pool assets. CFR prev | next § 229.1111 (Item 1111) Pool assets. Describe the pool assets, including the information required by this Item 1111. Present statistical information in tabular or graphical format, if such presentation will aid understanding. Present statistical information in appropriate distributional groups or incremental ranges in addition to presenting appropriate overall pool totals, averages and weighted averages, if such presentation will aid in the understanding of the data. In addition to presenting the number, amount and percentage of pool assets by distributional group or range, also provide statistical information for each group or range by variables, to the extent material , such as, average balance, weighted average coupon, average age and remaining term, average loan-to-value or similar ratio and weighted average standardized credit score or other applicable measure of obligor credit quality. These variables are just examples and should be tailored to the particular asset class backing the asset-backed securities. Consider providing minimums and maximums when presenting averages on an aggregate basis and within each group or range. In addition, provide historical data on the pool assets as appropriate (e.g., the lesser of three years or the time such assets have existed) to allow material evaluation of the pool data. In making any calculations regarding overall pool balances, disregard any funds set aside for a prefunding account. (a) Information regarding pool asset types and selection criteria. Provide the following information: (1) A brief description of the type or types of pool assets to be securitized. (2) A general description of the material terms of the pool assets. (3) A description of the solicitation, credit-granting or underwrit
perturbation.justification: The change from 'at least two business days before' to 'no later than one business day after' the Acquisition Date introduces a contradiction because it could prevent timely compliance with disclosure requirements for asset-backed securities. The Law Explanation states that this impacts Regulation AB, specifically 17 CFR § 229.1111, by potentially compromising the ability to provide accurate and timely information to investors and regulators. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 17 CFR § 229.1111 and states that it concerns '(Item 1111) Pool assets' and requires a description of the pool assets. By delivering the Transfer Notice after the Acquisition Date, the ability to accurately describe the pool assets before the transfer occurs is compromised, making compliance more difficult and potentially violating the regulation.

perturbation.original_text: All filings (including UCC filings) necessary in any jurisdiction to give the Depositor a first priority, validly perfected ownership and security interest in the Originator Transferred Property and the Master Trust Transferred Property, to give the Issuer a first priority, validly perfected ownership and security interest in the Depositor Transferred Property and to give the Indenture Trustee a first priority perfected security interest in the Collateral, will be made within ten (10) days after the Closing Date or the related Acquisition Date, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: All filings (including UCC filings) necessary in any jurisdiction to give the Depositor a first priority, validly perfected ownership and security interest in the Originator Transferred Property and the Master Trust Transferred Property, to give the Issuer a first priority, validly perfected ownership and security interest in the Depositor Transferred Property and to give the Indenture Trustee a first priority perfected security interest in the Collateral, will be made within sixty (60) days after the Closing Date or the related Acquisition Date, as applicable.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the UCC filings from 10 days to 60 days after the closing date introduces a legal contradiction by increasing the risk that the security interests are not perfected in a timely manner, potentially violating requirements for prompt perfection of security interests.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 9-506 discusses the effect of errors or omissions in financing statements. While it doesn't explicitly mandate a strict timeframe for filing, delaying filing significantly increases the risk that a filing could be considered 'seriously misleading' due to intervening liens or changes in circumstances, thus jeopardizing the perfected status of the security interest. The original ten-day period implies an intent for prompt filing to minimize this risk, while extending it to sixty days significantly increases the likelihood of complications.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 9-506. EFFECT OF ERRORS OR OMISSIONS. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 9-506. EFFECT OF ERRORS OR OMISSIONS. (a) [Minor errors and omissions.] A financing statement substantially satisfying the requirements of this part is effective, even if it has minor errors or omissions, unless the errors or omissions make the financing statement seriously misleading. (b) [Financing statement seriously misleading.] Except as otherwise provided in subsection (c), a financing statement that fails sufficiently to provide the name of the debtor in accordance with Section 9-503(a) is seriously misleading. (c) [Financing statement not seriously misleading.] If a search of the records of the filing office under the debtor 's correct name, using the filing office's standard search logic, if any, would disclose a financing statement that fails sufficiently to provide the name of the debtor in accordance with Section 9-503(a) , the name provided does not make the financing statement seriously misleading. (d) ["Debtor's correct name."] For purposes of Section 9-508(b) , the "debtor's correct name" in subsection (c) means the correct name of the new debtor . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification from 10 to 60 days for UCC filings creates a legal contradiction. Although UCC § 9-506 doesn't explicitly mandate a filing timeframe, delaying the filing significantly increases the risk that the filing could be considered 'seriously misleading' due to intervening liens or changes in circumstances, jeopardizing the perfected status of the security interest, as the Law Explanation notes. The original ten-day period implied an intent for prompt filing to minimize this risk, while extending it to sixty days significantly increases the likelihood of complications. This inconsistency with the implicit requirement for prompt filing to ensure a validly perfected security interest constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) days following the earlier to occur of (i) a ratings downgrade by each of the Rating Agencies of Verizon to below investment grade, or (ii) a Servicer Termination Event, the Servicer will send a notice to all Obligors indicating (a) that their Receivables have been assigned to the Issuer, and (b)(x) if Cellco has not been removed as Servicer, that the Obligors shall continue to make their payments as they had previously, or (y) if Cellco has been removed as Servicer, the name of the Successor Servicer and any new instructions with respect to their payments.
perturbation.changed_text: Within thirty (30) days following the earlier to occur of (i) a ratings downgrade by each of the Rating Agencies of Verizon to below investment grade, or (ii) a Servicer Termination Event, the Servicer will send a notice to all Obligors indicating (a) that their Receivables have been assigned to the Issuer, and (b)(x) if Cellco has not been removed as Servicer, that the Obligors shall continue to make their payments as they had previously, or (y) if Cellco has been removed as Servicer, the name of the Successor Servicer and any new instructions with respect to their payments.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the notification period from 10 to 30 days after a ratings downgrade or Servicer Termination Event could violate consumer protection laws that require timely and accurate notification to debtors regarding changes in their loan servicing or ownership.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly applicable as this involves device payment plans, the spirit and intent of 12 CFR § 1024.39 (Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA)) regarding mortgage servicing transfers is analogous. This rule requires servicers to notify borrowers of a servicing transfer within a specific timeframe (15 days before and after), to ensure borrowers know where to send payments. By analogy, delaying the notification of the assignment of receivables, especially after a potentially adverse event like a ratings downgrade or servicer termination, may violate the general principle of timely communication to consumers about their financial obligations. It could lead to confusion and misdirected payments.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the notification period from 10 to 30 days. The Law Explanation analogizes this delay to RESPA rules (12 CFR § 1024.39) concerning mortgage servicing transfers, which mandates timely borrower notification. Although the scraped snippets do not provide the exact text of 12 CFR § 1024.39, the Law Explanation's reference to this law, combined with the Perturbation Explanation that this change 'could violate consumer protection laws that require timely and accurate notification to debtors,' indicates that the extended timeframe could contradict the principle of timely communication, potentially leading to confusion regarding payments. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction: 'when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.'
